nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for the combined Fried Hor Fun / Crispy Noodles with Seafood dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient data for stir-fried flat rice noodles with seafood and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for mixed seafood components. The input record's per-100g values (energy 156.25 kcal, protein 10.63 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 11.25 g) are consistent with HPB-range estimates for this dish category and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed values are not available in HPB or USDA for this specific composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Rice noodles / flat rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Fried Hor Fun / Crispy Noodles with Seafood as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (fresh/flat, GI ~61, medium category) as the dominant carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (600 g × 11.25 g/100 g = 67.5 g) / 100 = 41.2. The seafood, vegetables, and protein components moderate the overall glycaemic response but the noodle base drives the GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg per 100 g; a standard 600 g plate delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the 2000 mg daily limit for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce, ask for reduced-sodium broth, and limit portion size to no more than 300 g to keep sodium intake below 1050 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate base (flat rice noodles or deep-fried egg noodles, GI ~61) with low dietary fibre (0.63 g/100 g) and cornstarch-thickened gravy produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 41 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200 g, increase the seafood and Chinese greens proportion, request reduced cornstarch in the gravy, and pair the dish with a high-fibre side to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 1.88 g/100 g and cholesterol is 56.25 mg/100 g; the deep-fried crispy noodle variant generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the high sodium load (350 mg/100 g) poses additional cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the stir-fried hor fun variant over the deep-fried crispy noodle variant to reduce AGE exposure, request reduced oil and sauce, and limit portion to 300 g to keep saturated fat below 6 g and sodium below 1050 mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying of noodles generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which promote systemic inflammation; sodium at 350 mg/100 g also approaches the RA caution threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request steamed or stir-fried flat rice noodles instead of the deep-fried crispy variant to eliminate AGE formation, and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Hor Fun / Crispy Noodles with Seafood is a classic Singaporean Zi Char dish available in two main variants: (1) stir-fried flat rice noodles (hor fun) coated in a rich, savoury egg-and-cornstarch-thickened gravy, and (2) deep-fried crispy egg noodles served with the same seafood gravy poured over the top. Both variants feature an assortment of fresh seafood — typically prawns, squid, and crab — along with Chinese greens such as chye sim, kai lan, or choy sum, seasoned with oyster sauce, soy sauce, ginger, and garlic. The dish is a staple of Zi Char (煮炒) hawker and restaurant cooking in Singapore and Malaysia, prized for its wok hei aroma, silky gravy, and generous seafood topping. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 156 kcal, 10.6 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 11.3 g carbohydrate with only 0.63 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, high-protein dish with a relatively high glycaemic load per serving due to the refined noodle base. Sodium is elevated at 350 mg/100 g, primarily from oyster sauce and soy sauce. The dish is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification; it poses moderate-to-high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and AGE content in the deep-fried variant. The seafood component contributes meaningful cholesterol (56 mg/100 g) and provides some omega-3 fatty acids. Chinese greens contribute vitamin C (2.5 mg/100 g) and iron (0.75 mg/100 g). This dish is commonly ordered as a shared main course at Zi Char stalls and restaurants across Singapore.

The GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flat rice noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and the absence of an exact HPB or USDA match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Zi Char seafood noodle dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. The dish has two preparation variants: stir-fried flat rice noodles (hor fun) and deep-fried crispy egg noodles; nutrient values represent a weighted average of both variants as served in Zi Char establishments. 3. The input record listed two portions both flagged is_default=true (800 g and 600 g); the 600 g portion is adopted as the standard serving for glycaemic load calculation, consistent with a single-person main course. 4. The input record's primary_cuisine was listed as 'Malaysian' but the dish is canonically Singaporean Zi Char; corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese (Zi Char)' with country_of_origin Singapore. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.06 g/100 g from the input record; this low value is plausible from cooking oil used in stir-frying and is retained. 6. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply.
